feedback.text: Me researching the digital economy regarding privacy and fairness, I strongly support the European Unions initiative to strengthen consumer protection in the digital economy. Considering growing concerns about monopolistic power, manipulative platform design, and addictive engagement tactics, it is vital to protect consumers, especially vulnerable groups. Addressing challenges like addictive designs in social media will also benefit the open web, including news publishers, which lost readers to social media. If implemented without careful calibration, however, the proposed regulation of dark patterns under the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA), combatting deceptive interface design and manipulative techniques, could jeopardize democracy and economic growth in Europe. In the EU, most news publishers rely on advertising revenue but are steadily losing advertisers to platforms like Google and Meta. One contributing factor is EU regulation that restricts the use of dark patterns in cookie consent banners. These rules have led to lower consent rates for tracking, which in turn reduces the effectiveness and transparency of advertising campaigns on publishers websites. While privacy-conscious consumers must not be pressured into granting consent, those who are less concerned about privacy and who recognize the value exchange should also not be unintentionally steered toward rejecting cookies. Democracy depends on independent journalism, which in turn depends on advertising revenues. Beyond news publishers, declining consent rates for third-party tracking also reduce the incentives for independent creators, such as bloggers in areas like travel or nutrition, to provide content. This weakens the diversity and availability of offerings on the open web and pushes consumers to spend more time on social media platforms, which are designed to be addictive and are known to foster polarization and the spread of radical content. Paradoxically, the Digital Fairness Act, while aiming to protect consumers from the harms of addictive social media use, could unintentionally accelerate these very harms by driving consumers away from the open web and deeper into social media environments. Moreover, the DFA risks becoming yet another layer of regulation that suppresses digital entrepreneurship in Europe. Previous privacy rules already placed heavy compliance burdens on digital start-ups and restricted their ability to use data; they in particular harmed advertising-supported business models. Now, by regulating dark patterns, the DFA could undermine both advertising-based and subscription-based revenue models. As a result, it may further erode the competitiveness of European digital start-ups and hinder the revival of a vibrant entrepreneurial ecosystem in Europe.
